content.text: When an organisation chooses the Partner path for a GenAI solution, the system integrator (SI) or technology partner produces a Statement of Work (SoW) with a fixed-price or time-and-materials estimate. That estimate covers only the partner's own billable time. The total cost of a partner-led integration — the figure that should appear in the TCO model — includes the partner fees, the internal oversight time, the underlying model or platform licence, and the compliance and security review costs. Without a complete cost model, partner integrations consistently run over the budgeted figure, and the partner path loses its apparent cost advantage over building.
Cost Components in a Partner Integration
A partner integration has six distinct cost components. The first two appear in the partner's SoW; the remaining four are borne internally and are frequently omitted from initial budget estimates.
All SI labour: discovery, architecture, build, UAT support, hypercare. Usually the largest single line item.
Ongoing enhancements, incident support, roadmap changes. Often structured as a minimum-hours retainer.
The licence for the LLM or platform the partner integrates. The partner uses it; the organisation pays for it directly.
Project management, partner coordination, architecture review, internal stakeholder communication. Cannot be outsourced to the partner.
Vendor due diligence on both the SI and the underlying model provider; security questionnaire; DPA review; penetration test if required.
Receiving the technical deliverable: code review, documentation review, runbook creation, on-call training for internal team.

# partner/cost_model.py
from dataclasses import dataclass, field

@dataclass
class PartnerEngagementCost:
    # Partner fees
    si_project_fee:        float   # fixed price or T&M estimate
    si_retainer_monthly:   float   # post-launch retainer
    retainer_months:       int     # how many months in the analysis horizon

    # Underlying platform
    model_licence_annual:  float
    licence_years:         int

    # Internal costs
    internal_pm_weeks:     float   # PM/tech lead time during project
    internal_hourly_rate:  float   # blended internal rate
    oversight_hours_per_week: float = 20   # 0.5 FTE default

    # One-time costs
    security_review:       float = 30_000
    knowledge_transfer:    float = 15_000

    @property
    def si_total(self) -> float:
        return self.si_project_fee + self.si_retainer_monthly * self.retainer_months

    @property
    def licence_total(self) -> float:
        return self.model_licence_annual * self.licence_years

    @property
    def internal_oversight_total(self) -> float:
        return self.internal_pm_weeks * self.oversight_hours_per_week * self.internal_hourly_rate

    @property
    def one_time_total(self) -> float:
        return self.security_review + self.knowledge_transfer

    @property
    def total_cost(self) -> float:
        return (
            self.si_total
            + self.licence_total
            + self.internal_oversight_total
            + self.one_time_total
        )

    def breakdown(self) -> dict:
        return {
            "si_fees":              self.si_total,
            "model_licence":        self.licence_total,
            "internal_oversight":   self.internal_oversight_total,
            "one_time_costs":       self.one_time_total,
            "total":                self.total_cost,
            "partner_pct":          round(self.si_total / self.total_cost * 100, 1),
            "internal_pct":         round(
                (self.internal_oversight_total + self.one_time_total) / self.total_cost * 100, 1
            ),
        }

# Example: 20-week RAG platform integration
engagement = PartnerEngagementCost(
    si_project_fee       = 242_000,   # $242k fixed-price SoW
    si_retainer_monthly  = 12_000,
    retainer_months      = 24,        # 2-year post-launch retainer
    model_licence_annual = 200_000,
    licence_years        = 3,
    internal_pm_weeks    = 20,        # full duration of project
    internal_hourly_rate = 75,        # $75/hr blended rate
    security_review      = 28_000,
    knowledge_transfer   = 20_000,
)

b = engagement.breakdown()
for k, v in b.items():
    if isinstance(v, float):
        print(f"  {k:25s}: ${v:,.0f}")
    else:
        print(f"  {k:25s}: {v}%")

# si_fees                 : $530,000
# model_licence           : $600,000
# internal_oversight      : $30,000
# one_time_costs          : $48,000
# total                   : $1,208,000
# partner_pct             : 43.9%
# internal_pct            : 6.5%   ← model licence (49.7%) dominates

Partner integration timelines are routinely underestimated. The SoW's stated timeline reflects the partner's own work stream. It does not account for internal review cycles, procurement delays, environment provisioning, and the inevitable scope clarifications that arise when the partner's understanding of requirements meets the organisation's actual data and systems.
Standard phase breakdown and risk multipliers
Stakeholder availability, incomplete requirements documentation, security review of access to production data
Internal architecture review cycles, cloud environment provisioning, approval for data access
Scope creep, integration surprises with existing systems, model behaviour requiring prompt re-engineering
Bug backlog from UAT, security findings requiring remediation, delayed pen test scheduling

# partner/timeline_model.py
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class PhaseEstimate:
    name:             str
    sow_weeks:        float   # partner's stated estimate
    risk_multiplier:  float   # typical actual / SoW estimate
    internal_buffer:  float   # additional weeks for internal processes

    @property
    def realistic_weeks(self) -> float:
        return self.sow_weeks * self.risk_multiplier + self.internal_buffer

PHASES = [
    PhaseEstimate("Discovery & Scoping",      sow_weeks=3,  risk_multiplier=1.5, internal_buffer=1),
    PhaseEstimate("Architecture & Design",    sow_weeks=3,  risk_multiplier=1.3, internal_buffer=1),
    PhaseEstimate("Build (sprints)",          sow_weeks=8,  risk_multiplier=1.4, internal_buffer=2),
    PhaseEstimate("UAT & Security Review",    sow_weeks=3,  risk_multiplier=1.5, internal_buffer=1),
    PhaseEstimate("Go-Live & Hypercare",      sow_weeks=2,  risk_multiplier=1.0, internal_buffer=0),
]

sow_total      = sum(p.sow_weeks for p in PHASES)
realistic_total = sum(p.realistic_weeks for p in PHASES)

print(f"Partner SoW timeline:  {sow_total:.0f} weeks")
print(f"Realistic estimate:    {realistic_total:.0f} weeks")
print(f"Contingency buffer:    {realistic_total - sow_total:.0f} weeks ({(realistic_total/sow_total - 1)*100:.0f}%)")

# Output:
# Partner SoW timeline:  19 weeks
# Realistic estimate:    31 weeks
# Contingency buffer:    12 weeks (63%)

Add 50–70% to the partner's stated timeline as a planning contingency. This is not pessimism — it is the observed ratio between SoW timelines and actual delivery timelines for enterprise GenAI integrations. Plan for the realistic estimate; communicate the SoW estimate to the partner as the target.

The SoW is the contractual document that governs the engagement. A poorly structured SoW is the primary cause of cost overruns and timeline delays in partner integrations. The following elements are required in any SoW for a GenAI integration.

Each deliverable must have written acceptance criteria that both parties agree to before work begins. 'A working RAG system' is not an acceptance criterion. 'The system returns a relevant citation for 90% of queries in the test set within 3 seconds at P95 latency' is an acceptance criterion.

Any scope change must go through a defined change control process that produces a written change order with an agreed cost and timeline impact. Without this clause, partners add scope informally and bill T&M for the additional work without the organisation having an opportunity to decline.

All custom code, prompt templates, configuration, and documentation produced during the engagement should be assigned to the organisation, not retained by the partner. SaaS-based partners sometimes attempt to retain ownership of work product built on their platform — this creates lock-in that is separate from the platform itself.

The SoW should include a specific knowledge transfer phase with defined outputs: code documentation, architecture decision records, runbooks, and a defined number of walkthrough sessions with the internal team. Without this, the organisation cannot maintain or extend the solution without the partner.

The SoW must specify how the partner handles organisation data during the engagement: which data they have access to, where it is stored during the project, how it is deleted after delivery, and whether subcontractors can access it. These provisions are separate from the DPA and should be in the SoW itself.

Post-launch partner retainers are often underestimated in the initial budget. A retainer that appears affordable in Year 1 can become a significant ongoing cost by Year 3, particularly if the retainer price escalates or if scope expands.

# partner/retainer_model.py

def model_retainer_cost(
    monthly_fee:          float,
    months:               int,
    annual_uplift_pct:    float = 10.0,   # typical retainer price escalation
    scope_creep_factor:   float = 1.15,   # observed: retainers grow ~15% in scope
) -> list[dict]:
    """
    Project retainer cost over a multi-year horizon accounting for
    price escalation and typical scope creep.
    """
    results = []
    current_fee = monthly_fee
    for month in range(1, months + 1):
        year = (month - 1) // 12 + 1
        # Apply annual uplift at the start of each year after the first
        if month > 1 and (month - 1) % 12 == 0:
            current_fee *= (1 + annual_uplift_pct / 100)
        results.append({
            "month":      month,
            "year":       year,
            "monthly_fee": round(current_fee * scope_creep_factor, 0),
        })
    return results

schedule = model_retainer_cost(monthly_fee=12_000, months=36)
for year in [1, 2, 3]:
    year_data = [m for m in schedule if m["year"] == year]
    annual_total = sum(m["monthly_fee"] for m in year_data)
    print(f"Year {year}: ${annual_total:,.0f} ({len(year_data)} months)")

# Year 1: $165,600   ($13,800/month × 12, with scope creep)
# Year 2: $182,160   (after 10% uplift)
# Year 3: $200,376   (after second 10% uplift)
# 3-year retainer total: $548,136
# vs. initial projection at flat $12k/month: $432,000
# Difference: $116,136 (27% higher)
Not all system integrators have equivalent GenAI capability. The partner selection process should evaluate technical competence specific to the solution type, not just general SI credentials.

Has the partner delivered a production RAG system, fine-tuning pipeline, or GenAI evaluation framework in the last 18 months? General ML or data engineering experience does not substitute for GenAI delivery experience.
Request two or three case studies with architecture diagrams; ask for references from the engineering lead on those engagements, not the account manager.

Is the partner a certified or preferred partner of the model provider you intend to use? Certified partners typically have access to technical support escalations, roadmap previews, and priority incident response.
Ask the model provider directly which SIs they recommend for this use case; verify the partner's status on the provider's public partner portal.
Will the same team that pitched the work actually deliver it? SI engagements frequently involve a senior team that wins the work and a more junior team that executes it.
Request the CVs of the specific engineers who will be assigned to the engagement before contract signature; include a clause that team changes require advance notice and approval.
Does the partner leave the organisation able to maintain and extend the solution independently, or does it create dependency on the retainer?
Ask for references specifically on knowledge transfer quality; ask whether the client's internal team can make changes to the solution today without partner involvement.
Is fixed-price or T&M more appropriate for this engagement? Fixed-price works when requirements are well-defined; T&M is appropriate when requirements will evolve during delivery.
Prefer fixed-price for phases with stable requirements (discovery output, architecture); T&M with a capped budget for the build phase if requirements are likely to evolve.

After go-live, a structured post-engagement review captures what the cost and timeline model got right and wrong. This information improves future partner integrations and informs whether the retainer is providing value.

Compare actual total cost (all six components) against the pre-engagement model. Identify which components were most underestimated. If model licence costs were 40% higher than projected, update the assumptions in the standard cost model for future engagements.

Compare actual phase durations against both the SoW estimates and the risk-adjusted estimates. Which phases ran longest? Which causes of overrun were predicted by the model and which were unexpected? Update the risk multipliers accordingly.

6 weeks post-launch, survey the internal team: Can they understand the solution architecture without partner involvement? Can they make minor changes independently? Have they needed to call the partner for something the runbooks should have covered? Deficiencies feed back into the next SoW's knowledge transfer requirements.

At the 3-month post-launch mark, review retainer utilisation: how many of the contracted hours were used, and for what purposes? If the retainer is primarily being used for defect fixes (work that should have been in scope), renegotiate scope or enforce the warranty clause. If it is being used productively for enhancements, the retainer is working as intended.

Partner integration cost and timeline models must account for six cost components (SI fees, retainer, model licence, internal oversight, security review, knowledge transfer) — not just the partner's SoW total. The SoW timeline should be multiplied by a 50–70% contingency to reflect the observed gap between stated and actual delivery timelines in enterprise GenAI integrations. A well-structured SoW with explicit acceptance criteria, change control, IP assignment, knowledge transfer deliverables, and data handling provisions prevents the most common causes of cost overruns. Post-launch retainers should be modelled with annual price escalation and scope creep factors; a retainer that looks affordable at $12k/month can cost $200k/year by Year 3. The complete cost model — partner fees plus all internal and ancillary costs — is what belongs in the TCO comparison, not the SoW total alone.
